questions.question: Which set of concerns belongs to **strategic** design rather than tactical design in DDD?
options: Bounded contexts, subdomains, ubiquitous language, and context maps
Entities, value objects, and aggregate roots
Repositories, factories, and domain services
Domain events and their post-commit dispatching
questions.explanation: Strategic design is the high-level work of splitting the domain into subdomains, defining bounded contexts and their ubiquitous languages, and mapping how contexts relate. Entities, value objects, aggregates, repositories, factories, domain services, and domain events are all tactical building blocks used inside a single context.

questions.question: The ubiquitous language is enterprise-wide: a given term like "Customer" must mean exactly the same thing across every bounded context in the system.
questions.explanation: The ubiquitous language is bounded — it is consistent only *within* one bounded context. The same word (Customer, Account, Policy, Product) can legitimately mean different things in different contexts.

questions.question: What fundamentally distinguishes an **entity** from a **value object**?
options: An entity has a stable identity that persists through state changes; a value object has no identity and is defined by its attributes
An entity is immutable, whereas a value object is mutable in place
An entity can hold behavior, whereas a value object can only hold data
An entity lives in a repository; a value object is always its own table
questions.explanation: Identity is the defining difference: entities are equal by id and stay 'the same' as fields change; value objects are equal by value and interchangeable. Option B reverses mutability (value objects are immutable). Value objects can hold behavior, and they are typically persisted as part of an aggregate (e.g. owned types), not necessarily a separate table.

questions.question: According to the aggregate consistency rule in DDD, what should a single transaction create or modify?
options: One aggregate
All aggregates referenced by id from the one being changed
Every aggregate within the same bounded context
Exactly two aggregates so changes stay paired
questions.explanation: The rule is 'one transaction = one aggregate.' Consistency within an aggregate is immediate; consistency between aggregates is eventual (often via domain events). Modifying multiple aggregates in one transaction violates the boundary and creates contention.

questions.question: Your team needs authentication, billing, and transactional email. In DDD subdomain terms, what is the recommended strategy for these?
options: Treat them as generic subdomains and buy or adopt an off-the-shelf solution
Treat them as the core domain and assign your best engineers to build them
Treat them as supporting subdomains and fully model them with tactical DDD
Merge them into one bounded context to share a single ubiquitous language
questions.explanation: Auth, billing, and email are classic generic subdomains — solved, common problems — so you buy or adopt an existing solution rather than reinvent. The core domain (your competitive advantage) is where you invest your best people and full tactical DDD.

questions.question: A new context must integrate with a messy legacy system whose data model is inconsistent. Which context-map pattern translates the foreign model into your own clean model so it can't leak in?
options: Anticorruption Layer (ACL)
Conformist
Shared Kernel
Open Host Service (OHS)
questions.explanation: An Anticorruption Layer is a translation layer mapping the upstream/legacy model into your own clean model, preventing the foreign model from corrupting yours. Conformist does the opposite — it adopts the upstream model as-is. Shared Kernel shares a model subset, and OHS is something an upstream publishes for consumers.

questions.question: What best describes the **Conformist** relationship in a context map?
options: The downstream context adopts the upstream model as-is with no translation, coupling itself to upstream changes
The downstream context's needs drive the upstream team's backlog, though upstream still owns its model
Two contexts share a small, explicitly agreed model subset that needs joint agreement to change
The upstream publishes a documented interchange format used by many downstream consumers
questions.explanation: Conformist means the downstream simply conforms to and adopts the upstream model with no translation — cheap, but it couples you to upstream changes. Option B is Customer–Supplier, option C is Shared Kernel, and option D is Published Language / OHS.

questions.question: Why should you reference **other** aggregates by identity (id) rather than by direct object reference? (Select all that apply.)
options: It keeps aggregates small and decoupled, allowing them to be loaded independently
It avoids accidentally loading huge object graphs into memory
It respects the one-transaction-per-aggregate boundary, since you don't pull a second aggregate in to modify it
It guarantees immediate strong consistency between the two aggregates
It removes the need for repositories entirely
questions.explanation: Referencing by id keeps aggregates small, decoupled, and independently loadable, avoids loading large graphs, and respects the transaction boundary. It does NOT give immediate consistency between aggregates (that consistency is eventual), and aggregates are still loaded and saved through repositories.

questions.question: How do a **domain service** and an **application service** differ in DDD?
options: A domain service holds business logic spanning entities/VOs; an application service is a thin use-case orchestrator with no business rules
A domain service orchestrates transactions and repositories; an application service holds the core business rules
Both contain business rules, but a domain service is stateful while an application service is stateless
A domain service is part of the infrastructure layer; an application service is part of the domain layer
questions.explanation: A domain service *is* business logic that fits no single entity or value object (stateless, in the domain layer). An application service is a thin use-case orchestrator (transactions, repositories, events) with no business rules. Option B reverses the two; both are conceptually stateless, and their layer placement is reversed in option D.

questions.question: An anemic domain model — domain objects with getters/setters but no behavior, with all logic in services — is considered an anti-pattern for DDD, yet it can still be a pragmatic choice for simple/CRUD subdomains.
questions.explanation: Fowler labels the anemic model an anti-pattern for DDD because it is procedural code that spreads invariants across services. Even so, it can be a pragmatic choice for simple/CRUD subdomains — you just shouldn't call that DDD.

questions.question: In DDD, how should repositories be scoped, and what do they operate on?
options: One repository per aggregate root, saving and loading the whole aggregate through it
One repository per database table, loading individual rows
One repository per entity, including non-root entities inside an aggregate
One shared generic repository covering every type in the domain
questions.explanation: A repository gives collection-like access to aggregate roots: one per aggregate root, not per table or per entity, and you load/save the whole aggregate through it. Non-root internals are reached only through the root, so they don't get their own repositories.

questions.question: When classic DDD layers are arranged as Clean/Onion/Hexagonal architecture, which statement about dependencies is correct?
options: Dependencies point inward; the domain depends on nothing, and infrastructure implements interfaces the domain defines
The domain layer depends on the infrastructure layer so it can persist itself directly
The application layer depends inward on infrastructure, which holds the business rules
All layers depend on a shared utility layer that contains the entities
questions.explanation: In Clean/Onion/Hexagonal architecture dependencies point inward; the domain depends on nothing, and infrastructure implements interfaces defined by the domain/application (Dependency Inversion). The domain never depends on infrastructure — that would invert the rule.

questions.question: Given this `Money` value object, which `Add` implementation correctly follows value-object semantics?
options: Option A — it returns a new `Money` instead of mutating, preserving immutability
Option B — mutating in place is fine because `Money` is small
Both are equally valid ways to implement a value object
Neither — a value object must never expose an `Add` method
questions.explanation: Value objects are immutable: you produce a *new* value object instead of mutating in place, exactly as Option A does with a `with` expression. (Option B also wouldn't compile against a positional record's init-only property.) Value objects may hold behavior such as `money.Add(other)`, so option D is wrong.

questions.question: Which statements about bounded contexts are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: A bounded context is a boundary within which one model and its ubiquitous language stay internally consistent
The same term (e.g. "Product") can map to different models in different contexts
A bounded context is a natural microservice boundary, with each service owning its model and data
All bounded contexts in a system must share one global model to avoid duplication
A bounded context guarantees that exactly one subdomain maps to it
questions.explanation: A bounded context keeps one internally consistent model/language, lets the same term differ across boundaries, and aligns naturally with a microservice. DDD explicitly rejects one global enterprise model. And while ideally one bounded context per subdomain, that mapping is not guaranteed.

questions.question: An aggregate raises a domain event so a second aggregate can react. What most accurately describes when the event is published and the consistency model between the two aggregates?
options: A dispatcher publishes it after the transaction commits, and consistency between the aggregates is eventual
It is published synchronously inside the same transaction, giving immediate consistency across both aggregates
It is published before the aggregate is saved, so handlers can veto the change
It is persisted as part of the second aggregate's transaction to stay strongly consistent
questions.explanation: Typically the aggregate raises the event and a dispatcher publishes it *after* the transaction commits; because a transaction touches only one aggregate, the second aggregate reacting separately means consistency between them is eventual. Publishing inside the same transaction to get immediate cross-aggregate consistency would violate the one-transaction-per-aggregate rule.

questions.question: A team designs a huge `Customer` aggregate containing every order, invoice, and support ticket the customer has ever had, all reachable through the root. What is the most likely consequence of this design?
options: High contention and loading large object graphs, since every change goes through one root and transaction boundary
Stronger decoupling, because everything the customer touches now lives in one place
Better eventual consistency, because all the data is strongly consistent in one transaction
Smaller transactions, because the root batches all of the writes efficiently
questions.explanation: Large aggregates cause contention (concurrent changes collide on the single root/transaction boundary) and force loading whole object graphs. DDD says keep aggregates small and link by id; the other options describe benefits a small-aggregate design provides, not this monolithic one.

questions.question: The `Order` aggregate must keep the invariant `Total == sum(lines)`. Which design best enforces it?
options: Option A — outside code mutates the aggregate only through root methods, so the root keeps `Total` consistent
Option B — public setters let callers update `Total` and `Lines` directly, which is simpler
Both work equally well, since the invariant is just simple arithmetic
Neither — invariants like this belong in an application service, not the aggregate
questions.explanation: The aggregate root must enforce invariants so the aggregate is always consistent; Option A exposes a read-only line collection and mutates only via `AddLine`, letting the root recompute `Total`. Option B's public setters let callers set `Total` independently of `Lines`, breaking the invariant. Invariant enforcement is a domain (aggregate) responsibility, not an application-service one.

questions.question: For which situations is investing in **full tactical** DDD most justified? (Select all that are good fits for heavy tactical DDD.)
options: A core domain with rich, evolving business rules and domain experts available to collaborate
A subdomain where the genuine complexity lives in the business, not just the technology
A mostly-CRUD supporting subdomain with thin, stable rules
A generic subdomain like authentication that an off-the-shelf product already solves
A technically-driven module whose hard parts are infrastructure, not business logic
questions.explanation: Heavy tactical DDD pays off when the core domain is genuinely complex, experts can collaborate, and the complexity is in the business. CRUD/supporting subdomains, generic off-the-shelf problems, and tech-driven modules add ceremony for little benefit, so you keep them simple — though strategic DDD can still apply broadly.
